---
title: "Recreating the S&P 500 and Dow Jones"
format:
  pdf: default
---

This section builds two **educational reconstructions** rather than exact official replications. The main simplifications are that we freeze the current constituent lists, use publicly available Yahoo Finance data, and approximate S&P-style market-cap weights with a shares-outstanding proxy. Because of that, the S&P reconstruction is best interpreted as an **S&P 500-style** index, while the Dow reconstruction is best interpreted as a **Dow-style** price-weighted index.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import yfinance as yf

plt.rcParams["figure.figsize"] = (10, 6)

UA = {"User-Agent": "IndexProject/1.0"}
start = "2019-01-01"
end = "2026-03-13"
base_value = 100.0
```

# Reconstructing an S&P 500-style index

We begin by downloading a current snapshot of the S&P 500 constituent list from Wikipedia and converting the ticker symbols to a format that is readable by Yahoo Finance.

```{python}
sp500 = pd.read_html(
    "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_S%26P_500_companies",
    storage_options=UA
)[0].rename(columns={"Symbol": "Ticker", "Security": "Company"})

sp500["YahooTicker"] = sp500["Ticker"].str.replace(".", "-", regex=False)

sp500.to_csv("sp500_constituents_2026-03-12.csv", index=False)

print("S&P 500 rows:", len(sp500))
sp500.head()
```

We now download daily historical prices for this frozen constituent snapshot. Since the constituent list is fixed at the present date and then applied backward through time, this reconstruction has survivorship bias.

```{python}
sp_tickers = sorted(sp500["YahooTicker"].dropna().unique())

sp_raw = yf.download(
    sp_tickers,
    start=start,
    end=end,
    interval="1d",
    auto_adjust=False,
    progress=False,
    threads=True
)

sp_close = sp_raw["Close"].dropna(axis=1, how="all").sort_index()

sp_close.to_csv("sp500_close.csv")

print("S&P close shape:", sp_close.shape)
sp_close.iloc[:5, :5]
```

To approximate market-cap weights, we also need a share-count proxy. The following helper pulls the available share history for each ticker and stores the result in a daily panel.

```{python}
def pull_shares_history(tickers, start=None, end=None):
    out = {}
    failed = []

    for ticker in tickers:
        try:
            s = yf.Ticker(ticker).get_shares_full(start=start, end=end)

            if s is None or len(s) == 0:
                failed.append(ticker)
                continue

            s = pd.Series(s).copy()
            s.index = pd.to_datetime(s.index).tz_localize(None)

            # Remove bad / repeated dates before concat
            s = s[~s.index.isna()]
            s = s[~s.index.duplicated(keep="last")]
            s = s.sort_index()

            if len(s) == 0:
                failed.append(ticker)
                continue

            out[ticker] = s.rename(ticker)

        except Exception:
            failed.append(ticker)

    if not out:
        return pd.DataFrame(), failed

    shares = pd.concat(out.values(), axis=1, keys=out.keys()).sort_index()
    shares = shares.loc[~shares.index.duplicated(keep="last")]

    return shares, failed

sp_shares, sp_failed = pull_shares_history(sp_tickers, start=start, end=end)

sp_shares = sp_shares.reindex(sp_close.index).ffill()
sp_shares.to_csv("sp500_shares_history.csv")

print("S&P shares shape:", sp_shares.shape)
print("Failed share pulls:", len(sp_failed))
sp_shares.iloc[:5, :5]
```

Our simplified S&P-style construction uses the approximate market capitalization

$$
\text{ApproxCap}_{i,t} = P_{i,t} \times S_{i,t},
$$

where $P_{i,t}$ is the closing price and $S_{i,t}$ is the shares-outstanding proxy. We then form the daily portfolio weight

$$
w_{i,t} = \frac{\text{ApproxCap}_{i,t}}{\sum_j \text{ApproxCap}_{j,t}},
$$

and use lagged weights to compute the next day's index return.

```{python}
common_sp = sorted(sp_close.columns.intersection(sp_shares.columns))

sp_close = sp_close[common_sp]
sp_shares = sp_shares[common_sp]

sp_market_cap = sp_close * sp_shares
sp_weights = sp_market_cap.div(sp_market_cap.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

sp_returns = sp_close.pct_change(fill_method=None)
sp_style_returns = (sp_weights.shift(1) * sp_returns).sum(axis=1, min_count=1).fillna(0.0)

sp_style_index = base_value * (1 + sp_style_returns).cumprod()
sp_style_index.name = "S&P 500-style reconstruction"

sp_style_index.to_csv("sp500_style_index.csv")

sp_style_index.head()
```

For reference, we also download the official S&P 500 price index series from Yahoo Finance and normalize it to the same starting value.

```{python}
sp_benchmark = yf.download(
    "^GSPC",
    start=start,
    end=end,
    auto_adjust=False,
    progress=False,
    threads=False
)["Close"]

if isinstance(sp_benchmark, pd.DataFrame):
    sp_benchmark = sp_benchmark.squeeze()

sp_benchmark = sp_benchmark.dropna()
sp_benchmark = base_value * sp_benchmark / sp_benchmark.iloc[0]
sp_benchmark.name = "Official S&P 500"

sp_compare = pd.concat([sp_style_index, sp_benchmark], axis=1).dropna()
sp_compare.head()
```

The next plot compares the reconstructed S&P-style series against the official index level. The two series will not match exactly because we are using a frozen present-day constituent list and a public-data shares proxy rather than the official float-adjusted methodology.

```{python}
ax = sp_compare.plot(title="Simplified S&P 500-style Reconstruction vs Official S&P 500")
ax.set_ylabel("Index level (base = 100)")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

We can also inspect the largest constituent weights in the reconstructed index on the most recent date in the sample.

```{python}
sp_name_map = sp500.set_index("YahooTicker")["Company"]

last_sp_weights = (
    sp_weights.dropna(how="all")
    .iloc[-1]
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(10)
    .mul(100)
    .round(2)
    .rename("Weight (%)")
    .to_frame()
)

last_sp_weights["Company"] = sp_name_map.reindex(last_sp_weights.index)
last_sp_weights[["Company", "Weight (%)"]]
```

# Reconstructing a Dow-style index

We now repeat the process for the Dow Jones Industrial Average. Again, we begin by downloading a current snapshot of the constituents and converting the ticker symbols into Yahoo Finance format.

```{python}
dow_tables = pd.read_html(
    "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dow_Jones_Industrial_Average",
    storage_options=UA
)

dow = next(
    df for df in dow_tables
    if {"Company", "Exchange", "Industry"}.issubset(set(map(str, df.columns)))
)

if "Symbol" in dow.columns:
    dow = dow.rename(columns={"Symbol": "Ticker"})

dow["YahooTicker"] = dow["Ticker"].str.replace(".", "-", regex=False)

dow.to_csv("djia_constituents_2026-03-12.csv", index=False)

print("Dow rows:", len(dow))
dow.head()
```

For the Dow-style construction, raw prices matter directly because the index is price-weighted. We therefore download both closing prices and stock-split data.

```{python}
dow_tickers = sorted(dow["YahooTicker"].dropna().unique())

dow_raw = yf.download(
    dow_tickers,
    start=start,
    end=end,
    interval="1d",
    auto_adjust=False,
    actions=True,
    progress=False,
    threads=True
)

dow_close = dow_raw["Close"].dropna(axis=1, how="all").sort_index()
dow_splits = (
    dow_raw["Stock Splits"]
    .reindex(index=dow_close.index, columns=dow_close.columns)
    .fillna(0.0)
)

dow_close.to_csv("djia_close.csv")
dow_splits.to_csv("djia_splits.csv")

print("Dow close shape:", dow_close.shape)
dow_close.head()
```

A simplified price-weighted index takes the form

$$
I_t = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_{i,t}}{d_t},
$$

where $d_t$ is the divisor. We start the reconstructed series at 100, then adjust the divisor on split dates so that the index level does not jump purely because of a stock split.

```{python}
dow_price_sum = dow_close.sum(axis=1)

divisor = pd.Series(index=dow_close.index, dtype=float)
dow_style_index = pd.Series(index=dow_close.index, dtype=float)

divisor.iloc[0] = dow_price_sum.iloc[0] / base_value
dow_style_index.iloc[0] = base_value

for i in range(1, len(dow_close.index)):
    today = dow_close.index[i]
    yesterday = dow_close.index[i - 1]
    split_row = dow_splits.loc[today].replace(0.0, 1.0)

    if (split_row != 1.0).any():
        synthetic_split_sum = (dow_close.loc[yesterday] / split_row).sum()
        divisor.iloc[i] = synthetic_split_sum / dow_style_index.iloc[i - 1]
    else:
        divisor.iloc[i] = divisor.iloc[i - 1]

    dow_style_index.iloc[i] = dow_price_sum.iloc[i] / divisor.iloc[i]

dow_style_index.name = "Dow-style reconstruction"
divisor.name = "Implied divisor"

dow_style_index.to_csv("dow_style_index.csv")
divisor.to_csv("dow_style_divisor.csv")

dow_style_index.head()
```

As before, we download the official DJIA series and normalize it to the same starting value.

```{python}
dow_benchmark = yf.download(
    "^DJI",
    start=start,
    end=end,
    auto_adjust=False,
    progress=False,
    threads=False
)["Close"]

if isinstance(dow_benchmark, pd.DataFrame):
    dow_benchmark = dow_benchmark.squeeze()

dow_benchmark = dow_benchmark.dropna()
dow_benchmark = base_value * dow_benchmark / dow_benchmark.iloc[0]
dow_benchmark.name = "Official DJIA"

dow_compare = pd.concat([dow_style_index, dow_benchmark], axis=1).dropna()
dow_compare.head()
```

The next plot compares the reconstructed Dow-style index with the official DJIA.

```{python}
ax = dow_compare.plot(title="Simplified Dow-style Reconstruction vs Official DJIA")
ax.set_ylabel("Index level (base = 100)")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

Because the Dow is price-weighted, the component weights are proportional to prices rather than firm size. The following table shows the largest price-based weights on the most recent date in the sample.

```{python}
dow_name_map = dow.set_index("YahooTicker")["Company"]

dow_weights = dow_close.div(dow_close.sum(axis=1), axis=0)

last_dow_weights = (
    dow_weights.dropna(how="all")
    .iloc[-1]
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(10)
    .mul(100)
    .round(2)
    .rename("Weight (%)")
    .to_frame()
)

last_dow_weights["Company"] = dow_name_map.reindex(last_dow_weights.index)
last_dow_weights[["Company", "Weight (%)"]]
```

The divisor itself is also worth examining, since it shows the mechanism used to preserve continuity through splits in the reconstructed series.

```{python}
ax = divisor.plot(title="Implied Divisor in the Dow-style Reconstruction")
ax.set_ylabel("Divisor")
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

# Comparing the two reconstructions

Finally, we summarize the behavior of the two reconstructed indices and their official counterparts.

```{python}
def summarize_index(series):
    rets = series.pct_change().dropna()
    ann_return = ((1 + rets).prod() ** (252 / len(rets)) - 1) if len(rets) > 0 else np.nan
    ann_vol = rets.std() * np.sqrt(252) if len(rets) > 0 else np.nan

    return pd.Series({
        "Start": round(series.iloc[0], 2),
        "End": round(series.iloc[-1], 2),
        "Total Return (%)": round((series.iloc[-1] / series.iloc[0] - 1) * 100, 2),
        "Annualized Return (%)": round(ann_return * 100, 2),
        "Annualized Volatility (%)": round(ann_vol * 100, 2)
    })

summary = pd.concat(
    [
        summarize_index(sp_compare.iloc[:, 0]),
        summarize_index(sp_compare.iloc[:, 1]),
        summarize_index(dow_compare.iloc[:, 0]),
        summarize_index(dow_compare.iloc[:, 1]),
    ],
    axis=1
)

summary.columns = [
    "S&P-style reconstruction",
    "Official S&P 500",
    "Dow-style reconstruction",
    "Official DJIA",
]

summary.T
```

These two exercises highlight the central point of index construction. The S&P 500-style reconstruction is driven by **firm size**, since larger approximate market capitalizations receive larger weights. The Dow-style reconstruction is driven by **share price**, so a high-priced stock receives more influence even if the underlying firm is not the largest company in the basket. That contrast is exactly why weighting methodology matters so much in passive indexing and benchmarking.
